evals.name: snapdragon-resnet-build
evals.prompt: i just got a snapdragon x elite dev box and i want to run microsoft/resnet-50 on the npu. can you walk me through getting it built and benchmarked? i want to see latency numbers at the end. give me actual commands i can copy paste.

assertions.text: Recommends `winml inspect` (or equivalent inspection step) before kicking off the rest of the pipeline.
Identifies QNN as the correct EP for Snapdragon X Elite.
Does NOT recommend OpenVINO, VitisAI, or other non-Qualcomm EPs as the primary path.
Walks through the build pipeline (export/optimize/quantize/compile or config+build).
Includes a benchmark step (winml perf or equivalent) that produces latency numbers.
Either directs the user to `winml --help` for current flags, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.
Includes an install/prereq block (or a clear pointer back to install steps) — the user said "just got a dev box" so they likely don't have winml installed yet.

evals.name: ryzen-ai-quick-benchmark
evals.prompt: I have an AMD Ryzen AI laptop and I want to know the fastest way to see how fast facebook/convnext-tiny-224 runs on my NPU. I don't really care about the artifact, just the number.

assertions.text: Identifies VitisAI as the correct EP for AMD Ryzen AI NPU.
Recommends a single `winml perf` invocation as the primary action.
Does NOT instruct the user to run export, analyze, optimize, quantize, and compile as separate steps.
Mentions running `winml inspect` first as a sanity check.
Either directs the user to `winml --help`, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.

Includes an install/prereq block (or a clear pointer back to install steps) — the user said "I have a Ryzen AI laptop" with no signal of prior winml usage, so the default-include-install rule applies.

evals.name: npu-vs-cpu-comparison
evals.prompt: i have a ViT model (google/vit-base-patch16-224) and im trying to decide whether to ship the NPU version or just use CPU. running on a snapdragon x elite. whats the cleanest way to get apples-to-apples latency numbers from both?

assertions.text: Recommends `winml inspect` (or equivalent inspection step) before any build/benchmark work.
Identifies QNN as the EP for Snapdragon X Elite NPU.
Recommends building the model once and then running `winml perf` twice — once on NPU, once on CPU — rather than building two separate pipelines.
Specifies that the CPU run should use the optimized (pre-compile) artifact, NOT the QNN-compiled artifact.
Warns that EP-compiled artifacts are EP-locked / can't be run on a different EP and produce nonsense numbers.
Either directs user to `winml --help`, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.
Includes an install/prereq block (or a clear pointer back to install steps) — the user's prompt does not signal prior winml usage, so the default-include-install rule applies.

evals.name: is-model-supported
evals.prompt: Before I commit to building `nvidia/segformer-b1-finetuned-ade-512-512` end-to-end on my Intel NPU, is there a way to know if it's going to make it through the full pipeline? Burned half a day last time on a model that exported fine but then died at the optimize stage.

assertions.text: Recommends `winml inspect` as the first, cheapest check (config-only, no weights download).
Recommends `winml analyze` after inspect — explicitly to find op patterns that won't survive optimize/quantize for the target EP.
Identifies OpenVINO as the EP for Intel NPU.
Explains analyze's role: a linter that classifies operators as supported / partial / unsupported per EP (so the user knows WHY this answers their question).
Does NOT just say "build the full pipeline and see what happens" — that's exactly the workflow the user already said burned them.

evals.name: optimize-failure-recovery
evals.prompt: i'm building microsoft/swin-large-patch4-window7-224 for QNN on snapdragon x elite. inspect and export went through fine but winml optimize keeps erroring with something about an unsupported op pattern. what do i do?

assertions.text: Recommends running `winml analyze` against the exported ONNX as the first diagnostic step.
Explains that analyze's linter will name the offending op pattern per EP (i.e., tells the user what analyze will tell THEM).
Does NOT recommend hand-editing the ONNX graph.
Suggests either trying a different optim/quant config to dodge the unsupported pattern OR escalating to "this model isn't a fit for this EP". Avoids dead-ending.
Encourages reading the actual error message (which names op/EP/stage) before guessing — doesn't jump to generic advice.

evals.name: install-setup
evals.prompt: i just got my snapdragon x elite dev box and i don't have winml installed yet. python 3.10 is already on the system. give me the actual install commands i should run to get going before i can do anything else.

assertions.text: Recommends Python 3.11 specifically, and calls out that the user's existing 3.10 will NOT work (winml-cli wheel pins >=3.11,<3.12).
Uses `uv venv --python 3.11` (or an equivalent isolated venv command) rather than installing into system Python or a 3.10 environment.
Recommends installing from PyPI via `uv pip install winml-cli` (or `pip install winml-cli`). Uses the correct package name `winml-cli` — does NOT install a generic `winml` package.
Includes a post-install verification step (e.g., `winml --help` or `winml sys --list-ep`) so the user can confirm the install worked before moving on.

evals.name: local-onnx-file
evals.prompt: i already have a resnet50.onnx file on disk from a previous export — about 100MB. how do i get it benchmarked on my intel core ultra NPU without re-downloading anything from huggingface?

assertions.text: Identifies OpenVINO as the correct EP for Intel Core Ultra NPU.
Recognizes that winml commands accept local `.onnx` files directly via `-m` / `--model` — no re-export needed.
Does NOT walk the user through `winml export` or any HuggingFace download — the model is already on disk.
Either directs the user to `winml --help`, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.

evals.name: config-build-for-ci
evals.prompt: we want to add a CI job that rebuilds an optimized resnet50 (targeting qnn) every time the model config changes. i want one json config that lives in the repo and a reproducible build step. how should i set this up?

assertions.text: Recommends the `winml config` → `winml build` two-step (the shortcut/pipeline path), not chained primitives (export/optimize/quantize/compile).
States or strongly implies the JSON output of `winml config` is the artifact to commit / version-control — it's the single source of truth for the build.

If precedence is mentioned at all, correctly states that explicit CLI flags override config-file defaults (per every primitive's --help: "Provides defaults; explicit CLI options take precedence"). Does NOT claim the config overrides CLI.

Addresses how to make the build repeatable in CI — either by mentioning that re-running `winml build -c config.json` against the same config produces the same artifact, OR by pointing at the `--rebuild` / `--use-cache` flags.
The CI workflow / runbook includes a winml-cli install step (pinned wheel today, PyPI later) — not just the build invocation. A fresh CI runner won't have winml; the workflow must install it.

evals.name: seq2seq-out-of-scope
evals.prompt: i want to deploy salesforce/codet5p-220m on my snapdragon x elite NPU for offline code summarization in a VSCode extension. the model takes a code snippet and outputs a natural-language summary. walk me through the winml pipeline?

assertions.text: Identifies CodeT5+ as a generative / seq2seq / encoder-decoder architecture (or otherwise invokes the body's scope rule about generative models). Must invoke a generalizable architectural property — not just say "we don't support codet5p specifically." Note: codet5p is NOT named in the description's exclusion list, so this tests whether the body's scope rule generalizes.
States clearly that this model is out of scope for the winml pipeline today, not hedged with "might work" or "try and see."
Does NOT walk the user through `winml export/optimize/quantize/compile/build/perf` for this model.
Suggests a legitimate alternative for running this kind of model on Windows/NPU (e.g., onnxruntime-genai, Olive, ORT directly) OR mentions roadmap for generative support.

evals.name: ambiguous-run-intent
evals.prompt: i want to run resnet-50 on my snapdragon x elite. how do i do that with winml?

assertions.text: Recognizes the prompt is ambiguous between 'just get a latency number' and 'produce a deployable artifact' — either asks the user to clarify, OR explicitly presents both paths with a reasoned recommendation. Does NOT silently pick one path without acknowledging the other exists.
Does NOT instruct the user to run `winml perf` first, then `winml build`, then `winml perf` again — that sequence wastes the on-the-fly build that perf already did.
If the response includes both a build step and a perf step, the order is `winml build` BEFORE `winml perf`, never perf before build.
Recommends `winml inspect` before other pipeline commands.
Identifies QNN as the EP for Snapdragon X Elite.
Either points the user to `winml --help`, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.
Includes an install/prereq block — the prompt gives no signal of prior winml usage.

evals.name: perf-output-recovery
evals.prompt: i already ran `winml perf -m microsoft/resnet-50 --device npu` on my snapdragon x elite and got latency numbers back. now i want to take that optimized onnx and ship it with my app. where did winml perf put the model file?

assertions.text: Correctly states that `winml perf`'s `-o` flag produces a perf metrics JSON, NOT the built model.
States that the on-the-fly artifact perf built lives in cache (or a temp folder with `--ignore-cache`) and is not directly exposed to the user as a deployable file.
Recommends running `winml build` with `-o <dir>` as the way to get a deployable artifact at a known location.
Notes that `winml build` needs a config file first (e.g., generated by `winml config`).
Does NOT fabricate a phantom path like 'perf saves the model at /tmp/...' or invent a hidden flag on `winml perf` that exposes the model.
Either points the user to `winml --help`, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns.
